_description: Master manifest aggregating all pipeline outputs. Single source of truth for the site.

key_findings: Across 9 Crimean ASNs and 90 sampled IPs, 120 successful geolocation lookups returned: 64 Ukraine (53.3%), 19 Russia (15.8%), 37 other (30.8%).

Per-ASN consensus: 4 ASNs resolve UA-dominant, 2 resolve RU-dominant, 0 tied, 3 no data returned. The split reflects the registration history: ASNs allocated to Ukrainian entities before 2014 tend to resolve UA; ASNs created after 2014 (notably AS201776 Miranda-Media) resolve RU because RIPE NCC registered them as RU at creation.

2 geolocation providers probed. The cleanest bright line in the pipeline: providers that follow BGP-derived data (like ip-api.com, MaxMind) inherit the RIPE NCC country code at registration time; providers that follow ISO 3166 (Cloudflare) resolve Crimea to UA regardless of who currently holds the prefix.

RIPE NCC's transfer policy ripe-733 treats ASN reassignment as an administrative transaction between holders, with no sovereignty review. Every Crimean ASN that resolved RU in this scan is the downstream effect of that single policy choice.
limitations: Sample size: 2 IPs per prefix, so total lookups scale with the number of announced prefixes per ASN (not a fixed count per ASN).
Tested from an EU/US vantage point; we do not currently cross-check from a Russian IP, which would reveal whether any provider serves different country codes to different requester regions.

ip-api.com and ipinfo.io are both BGP-derived to varying degrees; they share upstream data and are not fully independent. MaxMind and Cloudflare would be more authoritative ground truth but require paid API access.
Prefixes are hardcoded from RIPE NCC records as of the scan date. A full live RIPE STAT API integration is a follow-up.
A subset of IPs may be unallocated or dark — if an IP returns no geolocation data it is counted toward `errors`, not toward any country bucket.

key_findings: **ASN reassignment in action.** Of 9 ASNs historically associated with Crimean operators, **8 are no longer held by their historical operator** — they have been reassigned to different entities under RIPE NCC transfer policy `ripe-733` without any sovereignty review. Only 1 of the original 9 are still at their original holders. The reassignments include a transfer to Mobile Telecommunications Company K.S.C.P. (Kuwait's MTC), to UNINET (a Polish ISP), and to Yahoo-UK Limited — none of these entities are operating Crimean networks. This is the cleanest demonstration possible of the regulation gap: RIPE NCC has no sovereignty-review step and no mechanism exists to flag a Crimean ASN transfer as politically significant.

Live RIPE NCC country codes (9 ASNs): 2 currently registered RU, 4 currently UA, 3 in third countries (Poland, Kuwait, UK). 2 ASNs were created on or after 2014-02-20 (post-occupation). 1 have a .ru abuse email domain. Miranda-Media (AS201776, created 2014-07-16 with a .ru abuse email and RU country code) is the only clean example of 'created post-occupation as RU' in the probed sample.

All 3 Ukrainian mobile operators (Kyivstar, Vodafone Ukraine, lifecell) withdrew from Crimea by October 2015. They are classified `n/a` — the services no longer exist in Crimea — not `incorrect` and not `blocked`. The distinction matters: withdrawal is a Ukrainian operator decision under occupation, not a sovereignty claim about Crimea being Russian.

4 telecom entities operate in Crimea under Russian regulation and are classified `incorrect`: K-Telecom (Win Mobile, the de-facto monopoly operator since August 2014), RIPE NCC (which permitted the UA→RU ASN re-registrations under transfer policy ripe-733 without sovereignty review), the Kerch Strait submarine cable (laid by Rostelecom in 2014), and Miranda-Media (Rostelecom's Crimean data subsidiary, AS201776 registered as RU from July 2014).

3 services are `blocked` in Crimea: Starlink (SpaceX geofence, OFAC compliance), Netflix (OFAC since 2014), Speedtest.net / Ookla (blocked in Russia by Roskomnadzor as of July 2025). 'Blocked' here means the service is actively prevented from operating, not withdrawn by its operator.

1 service is `correct`: the `.crimea.ua` subdomain is active under Ukraine's .ua ccTLD, managed by Hostmaster.ua — an infrastructural assertion of Ukrainian sovereignty that has survived since before 2014.

Status taxonomy: `n/a` = operator withdrew (service gone). `incorrect` = service operates in Crimea under Russian regulation. `blocked` = service prevented by sanctions or state action. `correct` = operates per Ukrainian jurisdiction. These are NOT interchangeable — conflating `n/a` with `blocked` would misrepresent the withdrawn Ukrainian operators as victims of sanctions rather than as operators that left under occupation.
limitations: Curated operator findings (Kyivstar, K-Telecom, Netflix, etc.) are hand-researched from public sources. The RIPE NCC per-ASN registry probes are live.
Cannot directly query Russian operator coverage databases (sanctioned, requires Russian-IP access + manual browser session).
Submarine cable data is from public sources via TeleGeography; not all regional cables are mapped.
Ukrainian operators no longer publish Crimean coverage information (withdrawn), so current state is documented from withdrawal announcements.
WHOIS records can be edited by holders without external review, so RIPE NCC reassignment dates reflect the state at the date_checked timestamp in each finding.

findings.name: IANA Time Zone Database
findings.detail: zone1970.tab lists Europe/Simferopol as 'RU,UA' — both countries, RU listed first. This is the dual-listing compromise documented in the IANA tz mailing list: when Crimea switched to Moscow time in 2014, maintainers added RU without removing UA.

findings.name: Google libphonenumber
findings.detail: libphonenumber has dual Crimean encoding: 3 entries under +380 (Ukraine, ITU-valid) AND 2 entries under +7 (Russia). Additionally, the +7-978 carrier file lists 4 Russian mobile operators operating under a prefix Russia unilaterally assigned in 2014 and never submitted to ITU. Every Android phone, Chrome sign-up form, and phone validation library worldwide treats the Russian +7-978 prefix as canonical.

findings.name: OpenStreetMap Nominatim
findings.detail: All 6 Crimean cities returned country_code=UA

key_findings: **IANA Time Zone Database** lists Europe/Simferopol with country codes 'RU,UA' in zone1970.tab. zone1970.tab lists Europe/Simferopol as 'RU,UA' — both countries, RU listed first. This is the dual-listing compromise documented in the IANA tz mailing list: when Crimea switched to Moscow time in 2014, maintainers added RU without removing UA.

**Google libphonenumber** has dual-encoded Crimean phone prefixes: 2 entries under +7 (Russia) and 3 under +380 (Ukraine). The +7-978 Crimean mobile prefix — unilaterally assigned by Russia in 2014 and never submitted to ITU — lists 4 carriers as active: Elemte-Invest, K-Telecom Ltd, MTS, Sevastopol TELECOM. Every Android phone and browser validation library that uses libphonenumber treats this unilateral prefix as canonical. This is the 'Standards Silencing' pattern: ITU formally lists +380-65x for Crimea, but the validation layer that every downstream application actually consults has switched to the Russian numbering.

**OpenStreetMap Nominatim** resolves 6/6 tested Crimean cities to country_code=UA (0 to RU). OSM applies the 'on the ground' rule but the Nominatim geocoder currently returns the Ukrainian classification.

The three probes measure different layers of the same underlying question: timezone databases (OS-level), phone number libraries (validation-level), and geocoding (application-level). The IANA and libphonenumber findings together establish that Crimean sovereignty has been quietly downgraded from 'UA' to 'UA+RU' or 'RU' in foundational infrastructure without any standards body or international regulator objecting or being notified.
limitations: Live fetches against github.com (IANA + libphonenumber) and nominatim.openstreetmap.org — results are snapshots at scan time.
Nominatim rate-limits at 1 req/sec; the 6-city test takes ~8 seconds.
libphonenumber changes slowly but not never — the +7-978 carrier list can gain or lose operators between scans.
The 'on the ground' rule in OpenStreetMap is under active discussion in OSM's WikiProject Crimea. The Nominatim result is a snapshot of the current OSM community consensus.
Additional infrastructure systems (CLDR territories, ICU locale data, Unicode regional codes) are not yet probed by this scan.

findings.name: Natural Earth ne_10m_admin_1_states_provinces
findings.detail: Natural Earth's admin_1 file carries contradictory metadata for Crimea and Sevastopol. For Crimea, 7 fields assert Russian sovereignty (admin, adm0_a3, adm1_code, iso_a2, sov_a3, gu_a3, geonunit) while 7 fields in the SAME ROW carry correct Ukrainian metadata (iso_3166_2=UA-43, FIPS=UP11, GeoNames name='Avtonomna Respublika Krym', Yahoo WoE label='Crimea, UA, Ukraine'). The sovereignty assignment is not upstream inheritance failure — Natural Earth has the correct information in adjacent fields of its own record.

findings.name: Natural Earth ne_10m_admin_0_map_units

findings.name: PyPI — Python geo / visualization ecosystem
findings.detail: Combined weekly PyPI downloads of Python libraries in the Natural Earth chain: 34,084,085. Two layers: high-level libraries (geopandas / cartopy / folium / mapclassify / basemap / plotnine) at 6,840,269 weekly; and the C++ binding layer (shapely / pyproj / pyogrio / fiona / rasterio / gdal) at 27,243,816 weekly. The C++ binding layer is the universal Python entry point to GDAL, PROJ, and GEOS — every higher-level Python geo library (including geopandas, cartopy, and folium) goes through it. shapely alone, at ~15M weekly downloads, exceeds the entire npm visualization ecosystem combined.

findings.detail: Combined weekly Rust crate downloads in the Natural Earth chain: ~667,166 (estimated as recent_90d / 13). The Rust geo ecosystem is small but growing fast — geo-types and geo are the geometry primitives, gdal binds the C++ GDAL library, and geozero provides zero-copy I/O. Used by Rust GIS tools, edge-compute geospatial services, and the emerging Rust map server stack.

findings.name: Documented adjacent findings
findings.detail: Highcharts deliberate override + GeoPandas PR #2670 + 2022 consumer-vs-developer bifurcation — documented from public sources, not live-probed by this scan.

items.description: Highcharts is the only major visualization library in the npm ecosystem that ships a deliberate override of Natural Earth's Crimea classification. Their TopoJSON bundles for Europe and the World explicitly re-assign Crimea to Ukraine. No other major npm visualization library does this.

GeoPandas merged pull request #2670 in version 0.12.2 (late 2022) to correct the Natural Earth dependency behaviour for Crimea. Downstream GeoPandas users therefore get the corrected answer if they upgrade past 0.12.2.

After Russia's February 2022 full-scale invasion, consumer-facing platforms updated their Crimea classifications while developer infrastructure did not. Consumer side changed: Apple Maps (Crimea as Ukraine for non-Russian users), TikTok (Ukraine region separated from Russia), Booking.com / Airbnb / Netflix / Spotify (exited Russia). Developer infrastructure unchanged: Natural Earth SOVEREIGNT still Russia, Google Maps still shows disputed dashed border (unchanged since 2014), IANA tzdata zone1970.tab still 'RU,UA' (Russia first), and every major visualization library except Highcharts inherits the Natural Earth default.

key_findings: Natural Earth's admin_1_states_provinces.json carries internally contradictory metadata for Crimea and Sevastopol. Across the two rows combined, 14 fields assert Russian sovereignty (admin, adm0_a3, adm1_code, iso_a2, sov_a3, gu_a3, geonunit) and 14 fields carry correct Ukrainian metadata in the SAME ROWS — iso_3166_2=UA-43/UA-40, FIPS codes starting with UP (= Ukraine in the FIPS standard), GeoNames names in Ukrainian form ('Avtonomna Respublika Krym', "Misto Sevastopol'"), and Yahoo WoE labels that literally say 'Crimea, UA, Ukraine'. The sovereignty assignment is not upstream inheritance failure — Natural Earth has the correct information in adjacent fields of its own record.

Natural Earth's admin_0_map_units.json classifies the polygon containing Simferopol (44.95 N, 34.10 E) as SOVEREIGNT='Russia' with no NOTE_ADM0 footnote and no NOTE_BRK disputed flag. The top-level country polygon silently incorporates Crimea into Russia.

nvkelso/natural-earth-vector has 18 currently open issues mentioning Crimea, and 33 total items (issues + PRs, open and closed) over the repository's history. None of the open issues have been acted on by the maintainers.

Cross-ecosystem propagation measurement: combined live weekly downloads of libraries that consume Natural Earth across four package ecosystems — JavaScript (npm), Python (PyPI), R (CRAN), and Rust (crates.io) — total ~65,684,677. The Python total includes the C++ binding layer (shapely, pyproj, fiona, pyogrio, rasterio, gdal) that is the universal Python entry point to GDAL/PROJ/GEOS — every higher-level Python geo library goes through it. shapely alone (~15M weekly) is bigger than the entire npm visualization ecosystem combined. .NET (NuGet) doesn't expose weekly stats but NetTopologySuite alone has 190,182,977 cumulative lifetime downloads — it is the spatial backend of Entity Framework Core, used by every .NET application that handles geographic data. The chain also extends into desktop GIS (QGIS, ArcGIS, GeoServer, PostGIS, MapServer, GRASS), enterprise BI (Tableau, Power BI), and Java (GeoTools), Go, Julia, and MATLAB — not live-probed but all documented. The contribution of this pipeline is measurement of the scale, not discovery of the issue — the issue has been publicly raised for over a decade. The scale across the full ecosystem has not been measured before.

The deeper structural finding: the chain is rooted in GDAL/PROJ/GEOS — the C++ universal geospatial library stack that nearly every other ecosystem reads through. Python's geopandas → fiona → libgdal. R's sf → libgdal. QGIS, PostGIS, MapServer, GeoTools (Java), GDAL.NET, Rust's gdal-rs — all of them are language bindings on top of the same C++ implementation. Natural Earth distributes shapefiles, GDAL is the universal shapefile reader, and every geospatial application not written in JavaScript goes through GDAL to read them. The propagation chain is not 'JS / Python / R' as parallel ecosystems — it is one tree rooted at GDAL with language bindings as branches.

Highcharts (~2 million weekly npm downloads) is the only major visualization library that ships a deliberate override: its TopoJSON bundles re-assign Crimea to Ukraine. Every other library in the ecosystem ships the Natural Earth default unchanged. Existence proof that overriding is technically possible, and an editorial decision that 99%+ of the ecosystem has declined to make.

2022 bifurcation: after Russia's February 2022 full-scale invasion, consumer-facing platforms updated their Crimea classifications (Apple Maps, TikTok, Booking / Airbnb / Netflix / Spotify exited Russia). Developer infrastructure did not — Natural Earth SOVEREIGNT still Russia, Google Maps still shows a disputed dashed border unchanged since 2014, IANA tzdata still 'RU,UA' with Russia first, every major visualization library except Highcharts still inherits Natural Earth. The consumer side moved; the infrastructure side did not. That is the regulation gap measured across time.
limitations: Natural Earth is fetched from a public mirror (martynafford/natural-earth-geojson), which tracks the upstream repository but may lag by days. The authoritative upstream is nvkelso/natural-earth-vector.
Point-in-polygon test uses a single representative coordinate (Simferopol center). A full region test would enumerate every administrative polygon within the peninsula.

GitHub Search API counts 'crimea' keyword matches including historical discussions, not only issues explicitly requesting sovereignty correction. Manual inspection confirms the open set is dominated by correction requests.
npm download counts are weekly snapshots and fluctuate; the total represents the state at scan time (recorded in manifest `generated` timestamp).
The Highcharts override is documented from public Highcharts map-bundle files; we did not diff Highcharts' TopoJSON against Natural Earth's in this run.

2022 bifurcation items (Apple / TikTok / Booking / Airbnb / Netflix / Spotify changes) are documented from press reporting, not live-probed by this scan. The associated entries are labelled 'documented' in the manifest and should not be cited as freshly verified.

key_findings: Status taxonomy across 25 services: 12 correct, 3 incorrect, 4 URL-correct/UI-ambiguous, 0 worldview-compliant, 2 untested, 3 unreachable, 1 N/A.
Every 'incorrect' service is Russian-origin (Yandex Weather, Gismeteo, rp5.ru, Pogoda.mail.ru). No Western weather service classifies Crimea as Russian. The cleanest bright line in the entire audit.

Apple WeatherKit and Google Search weather panel are classified 'untested', not 'correct'. Both are suspected worldview-compliant (serve UA on EU/US IPs, RU on Russian IPs), but the hypothesis requires a signed Apple Developer JWT and a Russian IP proxy to verify. We refuse to label as correct without the test. This is a correction from the prior audit, which classified Apple as Correct.

Primary sovereignty signal defined: URL path (/ua/ vs /ru/). Secondary: <title>. Tertiary: breadcrumb/body text. Quaternary: timezone shown (Europe/Simferopol is the ISO-compliant choice; Europe/Moscow is the de-facto Russian choice). 3 services referenced Europe/Simferopol in probed HTML, 0 referenced Europe/Moscow.

Ground truth: GeoNames entry 693805 (Simferopol) live-fetched — country_code=UA. This is the upstream that every Western weather service's geocoding inherits from, and it is unambiguous per ISO 3166. The deliberate choice of Western weather providers to use GeoNames for geocoding (while most use OpenStreetMap for map tiles, where Crimea is dual-tagged under OSM's 'on the ground' rule) is the structural finding.
limitations: Tested from an EU/US network. Worldview-compliant services that switch on Russian IPs were not verified from a Russian proxy in this run — this is why Apple WeatherKit and Google Search weather panel are marked 'untested' rather than classified.
HTML scraping is brittle: some services return JS-rendered content or block non-browser user agents; those are marked 'unreachable' rather than being assumed correct.

The URL path signal is strong for services with per-country routing but meaningless for services that use GPS coordinates (e.g. Windy). For coordinate-routed services the classification falls back to title/body.
OpenWeatherMap's live geocoding verification requires an OWM_API_KEY environment variable; when absent, OWM is verified indirectly via the public web UI rather than the /geo/1.0/direct endpoint.

The timezone probe (Europe/Simferopol vs Europe/Moscow) scans HTML body for a textual reference; services that render the timezone via client-side JavaScript are counted as 'no tz in HTML' and not in either bucket.
Weather data accuracy (forecast quality) is not measured — only geographic / sovereignty attribution.

manifest.method: gdelt_bigquery + regex_stage1 + llm_stage2 + manual_stage3 (read from framing.json)
key_findings: GDELT corpus: 153,937 Crimea-mentioning articles indexed 2015–2026. Stage 1 regex classified 38,663 (25.1%) as containing any sovereignty signal.
Stage 2 LLM verification on 7,670 articles: 4,714 confirmed as genuine endorsements (61.5% precision, Wilson 95% CI [60.366%, 62.543%]).

Non-Russian-domain endorsements: 239 (0.545%–0.701% of 38,663 classified articles; 0.137%–0.176% of the full 153,937-article corpus). The prior fear that Russian narrative leaks into Western outlets is measurable, and the measurement is: it gets quoted, rarely endorsed.

Stage 1 non-Russian precision: 9.1% (Wilson 95% CI [8.06%, 10.262%]). About 90.9% of Stage 1's non-Russian 'russia-framed' flags are false positives — quotation, not endorsement. Naive keyword monitors would over-report Western 'violations' by roughly 10×.

Zero major international outlets (BBC, Reuters, CNN, NYT, Guardian, AP, AFP, DW, Le Monde, El País) appear in the LLM-verified endorsement list. Rule-of-three upper bound with n≈2626: true rate ≤ 0.114% with 95% confidence.

LLM independence firewall: the Stage 2 prompt contains only article body text — no domain name, no publisher country, no Stage 1 metadata. Stratified disagreement between Stage 1 and Stage 2 is 38.5% overall and roughly 91% for non-Russian articles — the LLM is performing substantive classification on article content, not rubber-stamping Stage 1.
limitations: This is an aggregator pipeline: it reads site/src/data/framing.json and site/src/data/media_violators.json produced by the upstream scripts (scripts/scan_gdelt_framing.py for GDELT + regex, scripts/llm_verify.py for LLM verification). It does not re-run either upstream on every invocation. To refresh the numbers, rerun the upstream scripts first.
GDELT coverage gaps for the most recent months may cause under-representation of newly published articles.
Paywalled articles are not fetched; open-web bias.
Domain-country attribution depends on GDELT metadata which is occasionally empty or mis-attributed.

The Wilson CI for Stage 1 non-Russian precision uses an inferred denominator (llm_endorses_nonru / precision_nonru). If the upstream framing.json rounded precision_nonru, the denominator may be off by a few units; the CI is still useful for magnitude.
Single-vantage-point LLM verification — all Stage 2 calls used the same Claude Haiku model. A cross-model verification against a second LLM would strengthen the precision estimates.

key_findings: 91,670 papers scanned via OpenAlex cursor pagination (2010-2026). 5,151 (5.6%) contain sovereignty-relevant signals.
LLM verification (Claude Haiku) classified 1,581 as Russia-framing, 2,131 as Ukraine-framing out of the signaled set.
Manual Stage 3 annotation of 1,605 papers confirmed 1,581 Russia-framing verdicts, yielding 98.3% LLM precision.
12 confirmed Q1 journals and 46 Q2 journals carry Russia-sovereignty framing for Crimea.
limitations: OpenAlex coverage depends on publisher metadata availability; some journals may be under-represented.
LLM verification uses abstract text only; full-text framing may differ.
Q1/Q2 journal rankings are based on Scimago Journal Rank at time of annotation.

findings.summary: 92 editions recognize Russian federal subject as standalone article; 100 recognize Ukrainian unit
UA/RU citizenship parity (24% vs 23%) among living Crimean-born people; prior 69% figure withdrawn

notes.post_2014_ru_passport: Number of people in the cohort with a P27=Q159 (Russia) edge whose qualifier P580 (start time) is on or after 2014-03-18. Unambiguously post-occupation passportization.
notes.post_2014_ua_ended: Number of people in the cohort with a P27=Q212 (Ukraine) edge whose qualifier P582 (end time) is on or after 2014-03-18. Explicit editorial record that Ukrainian citizenship ended under occupation.
notes.invisible_transition: Number of people with an explicit end-of-UA-citizenship edge (P27=Q212 with P582 >= 2014-03-18) but NO matching start-of-RU-citizenship edge (P27=Q159 with P580 >= 2014-03-18). Wikidata records the loss but not the gain — the 'invisible transition' pattern.
notes.caveat: Imperial Russia (Q34266/Q12544) and Soviet Union (Q15180) are separate buckets from modern Russia (Q159).

findings.summary: Erasure by omission in description fields (per-language counts)
11/17 Crimean entities have no current P17 country claim

key_findings: Entity-sitelink asymmetry: 92 Wikipedia editions have a standalone article for the Russian federal subject 'Republic of Crimea' (Q15966495) vs 100 for the Ukrainian 'Autonomous Republic of Crimea' (Q756294). 23 editions have the Russian entity but NOT the Ukrainian one; 31 have the reverse. The asymmetry in smaller editions is consistent with infrastructural normalization — editors created the Russian federal subject article in 2014 when it was in the news, while the legacy Ukrainian administrative unit was never written about.

Stratified Crimean-born people (N=577): among the 244 living or unknown-death cohort, UA-only citizenship 60 (24.6%) vs RU-only 58 (23.8%). Exact two-sided binomial test on the 118 people with exclusive citizenship yields statistical parity — p ≈ 0.93 for H0 of P(UA)=0.5.

Wikidata cannot represent post-2014 passportization: only 1 people across 577 entries have a P27=Russia edge with a P580 (start time) qualifier on or after 2014-03-18, and only 1 have a P27=Ukraine edge with a P582 (end time) qualifier on or after that date. The 'invisible transition' count — end-of-UA marked but no matching start-of-RU — is 0. Russia issued an estimated 2 million passports in Crimea after 2014; Wikidata records almost none of them as structured events. The data gap is the finding.
English Wikipedia description-field erasure: 30 'city in Crimea' (no country) classifications across all tested editions, vs 10 mentioning Ukraine and 0 mentioning Russia.
Wikidata P17 (country) coverage for 17 Crimean entities: 3 list Ukraine, 4 list Russia, 11 have no current country claim.
limitations: Tested 12 major language editions for description text; the sitelink sweep covers all editions that have any Crimea-related article.
Stratification bucket 'alive_or_unknown' includes entries with no P570 death date — Wikidata does not reliably record 'alive' as a distinct state from 'unknown'.
P580 (citizenship start-time) qualifier is rarely populated on P27 edges; absence of the qualifier is not evidence that citizenship was acquired before 2014.
Entity sitelinks count standalone articles only — editions that cover both topics in a single combined article are counted as 'peninsula only', not 'both'.
Description-text and category classification uses surface string matching; fine-grained semantic analysis of full article bodies was not performed.

findings.name: ISO 3166-2 / CLDR
findings.detail: ISO 3166-2 lists Crimea exclusively under Ukraine (UA-43 'Avtonomna Respublika Krym' and UA-40 'Sevastopol'). key_findings: 9 of 10 institutional systems classify Crimea as Ukrainian territory. The regulation gap documented in the rest of this audit is not a failure of the legal or institutional layer — every authoritative system that classifies Crimea gets it right. The gap exists in the downstream technical infrastructure that ignores these classifications.

OFAC SDN list: 31 Crimean place-of-birth records, 25 classified as Ukraine, 5 as Russia (for individuals born in mainland Russia, not Crimea). OFAC's Crimea sanctions program is titled Executive Order 13685 'Crimea Region of Ukraine' — the program name itself is a sovereignty statement.

ROR + OpenAlex: 4 of 5 Crimean academic institutions registered as UA, 1 as RU. The one RU-registered institution (Research Institute of Agriculture of Crimea) is also the institution that produces the largest number of 'Republic of Crimea, Russia' papers in OpenAlex — see the academic pipeline for the paper-level finding.

ISO 3166-2 has zero Crimean codes under Russia (RU has 83 federal subdivisions, none of them include Crimea). In November 2014 the ISO 3166 Maintenance Agency renamed UA-43 from 'Respublika Krym' to 'Avtonomna Respublika Krym', explicitly reinforcing the Ukrainian autonomous-republic framing.

ITU has not reassigned +380-65x from Ukraine to Russia. Russia created +7-365x and +7-978 unilaterally in 2014 — neither was submitted to ITU for assignment. ICAO Doc 7910 maintains UKFF and UKFB for Crimean airports; Russia's internal URFF code is not ICAO-recognized.

Library of Congress LCSH: 4 subject headings mention Ukraine, 4 mention Russia without Ukraine. The canonical form is 'Crimea (Ukraine)' and 'Crimea (Ukraine)--History--Russian occupation, 2014-' is an explicit US government library classification of the 2014 events as occupation.
limitations: EU Financial Sanctions Database requires browser authentication (HTTP 403 on direct download); EU-side evidence is the EUR-Lex regulation text, not the database.
US Congress API requires a registered key; US legislation evidence is OFAC (which is live-fetched) plus documented acts.
ICAO Doc 7910 is published as a paid PDF; airport codes are cross-referenced with IATA and Wikipedia. An authoritative diff against the paid Doc 7910 would require purchase.
ISO 3166 sells the standard document; verification uses the Unicode CLDR mirror (the technical implementation used by every browser and OS) which is open-source.

ROR coverage of Crimean institutions is extensive but not exhaustive. Some smaller or newer institutions may be missing from the registry entirely, which is itself a form of invisibility not captured by a UA/RU count.

key_findings: Cross-lab RLHF gap: all 8 flagship models show positive D-R gap (+0.05 to +0.33), meaning forced-choice sovereignty alignment exceeds free-recall alignment. Strongest gap: gemini-2.5-pro (+0.33), gpt-5.4 (+0.27).
Negative-gap inversion on 10 open/small models: open-ended recall is more Ukraine-aligned than forced-choice answers, suggesting RLHF training overcorrects toward neutrality.
4-tier SAS (Sovereignty Alignment Score) with 6 weight schemes shows rank-order stability (Spearman > 0.9 across schemes).

temp=0 does not guarantee determinism across all providers; seed=42 used where supported.

findings.detail: Three episodes form a pattern. (1) The 2016 Havana Declaration -- the first-ever Pope-Patriarch meeting -- deplored 'hostility in Ukraine' without naming Crimea, two years after its annexation. (2) On 25 August 2023, Pope Francis told Russian Catholic youth: 'You are heirs of the great Russia -- the great Russia of saints, of kings, the great Russia of Peter the Great, Catherine II.' (3) In February 2024, Francis said Ukraine should have 'the courage of the white flag' to negotiate with Russia. The Vatican later clarified this was 'a call for negotiations, not surrender.'

findings.detail: On 4 December 2014, Putin declared in his state-of-the-nation address: 'Crimea has invaluable civilisational and even sacral importance for Russia, like the Temple Mount in Jerusalem for the followers of Islam and Judaism.' This frames the illegal annexation as a religious mandate. The claim rests on the tradition that Prince Vladimir was baptized at Chersonesus in Crimea in 988 AD -- though Ukraine has an equal (and arguably stronger) historical claim to the same event. In 2015, Putin placed Chersonesus under direct federal control. In July 2024, Russia opened the 'New Chersonesos' complex on the UNESCO World Heritage Site (inscribed 2013). The project was implemented by the Moscow Patriarchate's My History Foundation; the Russian Ministry of Defence served as general contractor. Approximately 85,000 m2 of cultural layers were destroyed and ~4 million artifacts removed from Crimea.

findings.detail: Under Russian occupation law, Crimean religious communities must re-register under Russian Federation law and add language to their founding documents affirming that Crimea is part of Russia. Communities that refuse lose legal standing, access to their buildings, and face prosecution. Result: Of 46 Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU) parishes in Crimea at the time of annexation (2014), zero remain as of 2024. The last OCU church was physically demolished in June 2024. Non-Orthodox communities (Jehovah's Witnesses, Ukrainian Greek Catholics, some Muslim and Protestant groups) face bans, surveillance, and prosecutions. The practice of abduction, torture, and extrajudicial treatment of clerics from non-Moscow Patriarchate confessions is documented by RISU and international observers.

key_findings: The Moscow Patriarchate's June 2022 creation of the 'Metropolitanate of Crimea' is an ecclesiastical annexation that mirrors the political one. Three dioceses (Dzhankoy, Simferopol, Feodosiya) were unilaterally transferred from the Ukrainian Orthodox Church to direct Moscow jurisdiction. Putin's spiritual adviser Tikhon (Shevkunov) was appointed Metropolitan of Crimea in October 2023.

The 2018 Moscow-Constantinople schism -- the largest in Orthodox Christianity since 1054 -- was triggered by Ukrainian autocephaly. Moscow's break with the Ecumenical Patriarchate is a direct consequence of the sovereignty contest over Ukrainian (including Crimean) ecclesiastical territory.

The WCC has never addressed Crimea's sovereignty. As the ROC's largest institutional platform in the ecumenical world (~100 million adherents), the WCC's silence on Crimea specifically -- even as it condemned the broader invasion -- constitutes a significant gap in international religious governance.

The Vatican's engagement follows a pattern of omission: the 2016 Havana Declaration avoided Crimea, the 2023 'great Russia' speech invoked the empress who annexed Crimea, and the 2024 'white flag' remark implied Ukraine should negotiate without preconditions on territorial integrity.

Putin's 'sacred land' doctrine (Dec 2014) explicitly frames Crimea's annexation as a religious mandate, comparing it to the Temple Mount. The 2024 New Chersonesos complex -- built by the Moscow Patriarchate's foundation with the Ministry of Defence as contractor -- is the physical realization of this doctrine, at the cost of ~85,000 m2 of UNESCO-protected archaeological heritage.

Religious registration requirements in occupied Crimea function as a sovereignty-affirmation tool: communities must formally state Crimea is part of Russia or lose legal standing. This has contributed to the complete elimination of the OCU from the peninsula (46 parishes in 2014, zero in 2024).
limitations: This is a curated research pipeline, not an automated scanner. Findings are drawn from the briefing document, academic literature, verified news reporting, and human-rights documentation.
The 85,000 m2 destruction figure for New Chersonesos comes from Ukrainian government and heritage-monitoring sources. Russia disputes the characterization of the construction as 'destruction.'
The WCC Assembly vote count (unanimously defeated) is reported by multiple news sources but the WCC itself has not published a formal tally.

Vatican diplomatic language is deliberately ambiguous by design. The characterizations here ('omission,' 'pattern') are analytical interpretations supported by Ukrainian government and Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church reactions.
The ROC's membership count (~100 million) is a self-reported figure. Independent surveys (e.g., Levada Center) suggest significantly lower active participation rates.

субъектами Российской Федерации. РЕКЛАМА РЕКЛАМА Впервые Крымский полуостров вошёл в состав Российской Империи в 1783 г., в том же году был основан Севастополь, который стал военно-морской базой российского Черноморского флота. В 1954 г. по инициативе Первого секретаря ЦК КПСС Никиты Хрущёва и по случаю 300-летия воссоединения Украины с Россией Верховный Совет СССР передал Крым из состава РСФСР в состав Украинской ССР. В мае 1992 г. Верховный Совет Крыма принял конституцию Республики Крым и акт о её самостоятельности. Конституция была отменена в 1995 г. решением Киева. Россия сохранила за собой военно-морскую базу в Севастополе, которая оставалась главным местом базирования Черноморского флота. Сам город получил особый статус и не входил в состав Республики Крым. На 2013 г. в севастопольском порту базировались 60 военных кораблей российского ВМФ и служили 11 тыс российских военных. Свержение президента Украины Виктора Януковича в феврале 2014 г. вызвало массовые протесты жителей Крыма и Севастополя. В ночь с 26 на 27 февраля при активном участии российского спецназа произошла смена региональной исполнительной власти. 1 марта новое руководство Крыма обратилось к России с официальной просьбой «об оказании содействия в обеспечении мира и спокойствия на территории Автономной Республики Крым». Тогда же было принято решение о проведении референдума о статусе полуострова. Декларации о независимости Автономной Республики Крым и города Севастополя были приняты местными законодательными органами 11 марта. Пять дней спустя прошёл референдум, на котором было принято решение о присоединении к России. Российские военные и крымская самооборона заблокировали военные объекты украинских вооружённых сил, которые не оказали сопротивления и в конце концов были эвакуированы с территории полуострова. Участие российских военных в крымских событиях было признано Россией только после официального присоединения Крыма. Украина объявила Крым «временно оккупированной территорией» и начала поэтапно вводить меры его экономической блокады. В октябре 2015 г. неизвестные подорвали на территории Херсонской области опоры ЛЭП, временно обесточив Крым, а активисты крымскотатарского «Меджлиса» и украинские националисты ввели «гражданскую блокаду» полуострова. В апреле 2016 г. президент Украины Пётр Порошенко заявил о невозможности возвращения Крыма военным путём, но в августе военная активность у границы резко возросла. signals: субъектами Российской Федерации состояние здоровья призывников значительно лучше. Кроме того, по данным крымского военкомата, ежегодно растет число девушек, желающих служить в армии по контракту, что объясняется хорошей социальной базой, возможностью получения профессии и образования за счет средств Министерства обороны. Уклонистов от службы в армии, по данным Сергея Погорелова, сегодня в Крыму практически нет. Напомним, призывной возраст в России от 18 до 27 лет. Призвавшиеся в 2015 году крымчане будут служить только в военных частях, расположенных на территории полуострова.
